subject: ap-government
title: Concept Application — California Governor Recall and Arnold Schwarzenegger
stimulus:
In 2003, California's Democratic governor Gray Davis' popularity was decreasing due to a budget shortfall and an energy crisis that received widespread coverage in the news. As a result, Republicans in the state initiated a recall election of the governor by gathering over one million signatures.

Recall is an electoral process by which citizens can vote to remove elected officials before the end of their term. There are provisions for recall elections of state officials in 19 states. In California, citizens initiate a recall election by collecting a minimum number of signatures on a petition, which puts the issue on a ballot in a subsequent election. If a majority of the voters choose to recall the elected official, then that official is removed from office. In California, the recall ballot asks voters two separate questions: Should the governor be recalled? And if so, who should be the new governor?

The front-running candidate to replace Governor Davis was action movie star Arnold Schwarzenegger, a Republican. Schwarzenegger announced his candidacy on a popular late-night television program, and his celebrity status drew national media attention. During his appearance, he said of Davis, "He's failing [Californians] terribly, and this is why he needs to be recalled." With the understanding that California had more registered Democrats than Republicans, Schwarzenegger strategically focused his campaign on his own personal story as a self-made immigrant and distanced himself from national Republican leaders.

In the final vote, Californians voted to recall Governor Davis, and Schwarzenegger was elected as his replacement. Exit polls showed that most voters who favored recalling Davis cited his recent performance as governor as their reason.

parts.prompt: Describe the model of voting behavior that best reflects most voters' choice to recall the governor in the scenario.
parts.scoring_notes: Only the retrospective voting model earns the point. The response must explain what the model involves (evaluating past performance), not just name it.

scoring_points.description: Describe the model of voting behavior that best reflects most voters' choice to recall the governor in the scenario.

required_elements: Most voters used the retrospective model of voting behavior which considers Governor Davis's past performance when deciding how to vote.
alternatives.correct_example: Most voters used the retrospective model of voting behavior which considers Governor Davis's past performance when deciding how to vote.

required_elements: The model takes into account how voters tend to consider elected officials' past performance when deciding how to vote.
alternatives.correct_example: The model takes into account how voters tend to consider elected officials' past performance when deciding how to vote.

scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for describing the model of voting behavior that best reflects most voters' choice to recall the governor. Acceptable descriptions include: Most voters used the retrospective model of voting behavior which considers Governor Davis's past performance when deciding how to vote. The model takes into account how voters tend to consider elected officials' past performance when deciding how to vote.

sample_responses.response: Most voters used the retrospective model of voting behavior — they considered Governor Davis's past performance (the budget shortfall and energy crisis) when deciding whether to recall him.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies and describes retrospective voting — evaluating an elected official's past record — as the model shown by voter behavior in the scenario. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: Voters used the prospective model because they hoped Schwarzenegger would perform better as governor.
sample_responses.commentary: The scenario says most recall voters cited Davis's recent performance as their reason — that is retrospective voting, not prospective. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: In the context of the scenario, explain how the media's role as a linkage institution might have affected the voting behavior described in part A.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must connect media as a linkage institution to the specific retrospective voting behavior described in part A. Generic statements about media influence without connecting to the scenario do not earn the point.

scoring_points.description: In the context of the scenario, explain how the media's role as a linkage institution might have affected the voting behavior described in part A.

required_elements: 1 pt: Explains how media influenced the retrospective voting behavior. Accept: the media's reporting on Governor Davis's performance and his responsibility for the bad economy might have persuaded others to vote to recall Davis; media coverage brought attention to the energy crisis and budget shortfall, shaping voters' retrospective evaluation of Davis's record.
alternatives.correct_example: The media's extensive coverage of the energy crisis and budget shortfall under Governor Davis shaped voters' retrospective evaluation of his performance, making them more likely to support the recall based on his record.

scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the media's role as a linkage institution might have affected the voting behavior described in part A. Acceptable explanations include: The media's reporting on Governor Davis's performance and his responsibility for the bad economy might have persuaded others to vote to recall Davis. Media coverage brought attention to the energy crisis and budget shortfall, shaping voters' retrospective evaluation of Davis's record.

sample_responses.response: The media's widespread coverage of the energy crisis and the budget shortfall framed Governor Davis as responsible for those failures, which shaped voters' retrospective evaluation of his performance and made them more likely to support his recall.
sample_responses.commentary: Connects the media's role as a linkage institution directly to voters' retrospective evaluation — the media's reporting on Davis's performance influenced how voters judged his record. Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The media helped people learn about the recall election, so more people voted.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes the media increasing voter turnout generally, but does not explain how media coverage shaped the retrospective voting behavior (evaluation of Davis's past performance) described in part A. 0 pts.

parts.prompt: Explain how the electoral process for removing Governor Davis is different from the process for removing a sitting president of the United States.
parts.scoring_notes: The response must identify a specific procedural difference between the recall process and the federal presidential removal process. Accept any accurate comparison of at least one difference.

scoring_points.description: Explain how the electoral process for removing Governor Davis is different from the process for removing a sitting president of the United States.

required_elements: In the scenario, a simple majority of voters is required to remove the governor. A sitting U.S. president is removed with a two-thirds majority in the U.S. Senate.
alternatives.correct_example: In the scenario, a simple majority of voters is required to remove the governor. A sitting U.S. president is removed with a two-thirds majority in the U.S. Senate.

required_elements: In the scenario, the process of removing Governor Davis began when citizens initiated a recall by collecting signatures. To remove a sitting president, the House begins the impeachment process by filing formal charges, which must be approved by a majority of the House.
alternatives.correct_example: In the scenario, the process of removing Governor Davis began when citizens initiated a recall by collecting signatures. To remove a sitting president, the House begins the impeachment process by filing formal charges, which must be approved by a majority of the House.

required_elements: The process to remove the governor in the scenario is controlled by voters, while the impeachment process to remove a sitting U.S. president is controlled by elected representatives.
alternatives.correct_example: The process to remove the governor in the scenario is controlled by voters, while the impeachment process to remove a sitting U.S. president is controlled by elected representatives.

scoring_points.official_rubric: One point is earned for explaining how the electoral process for removing Governor Davis is different from the process for removing a sitting president of the United States. Acceptable explanations include: In the scenario, a simple majority of voters is required to remove the governor. A sitting U.S. president is removed with a two-thirds majority in the U.S. Senate. In the scenario, the process of removing Governor Davis began when citizens initiated a recall by collecting signatures. To remove a sitting president, the House begins the impeachment process by filing formal charges, which must be approved by a majority of the House. The process to remove the governor in the scenario is controlled by voters, while the impeachment process to remove a sitting U.S. president is controlled by elected representatives.

sample_responses.response: Governor Davis was removed through a recall election initiated by citizens collecting signatures, where a simple majority vote removed him from office. A sitting U.S. president cannot be removed this way — the president can only be removed through the impeachment process, which requires a majority in the House to impeach and then a two-thirds majority in the Senate to convict and remove.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately contrasts the citizen-initiated recall process with the congressional impeachment process, noting key procedural differences (who initiates and the vote threshold). Earns the point.

sample_responses.response: The recall is a state process, while removing the president is a federal process.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague — does not explain any specific procedural difference between the recall and the impeachment/removal process. 0 pts.
